notes.anchor: our Caelius is being attacked vigorously by the Clodian clan
notes.note: M. Caelius Rufus, Cicero's brilliant protege (whom he had defended in the Pro Caelio of 56 BC); the Clodian faction, kin and partisans of P. Clodius, were pressing a fresh prosecution against him.

notes.anchor: a crowded Senate gave audience to the Tyrians
notes.note: An embassy from the Phoenician city of Tyre was heard in the Senate, opposed by the publicani (tax-farming companies) who held the Syrian revenue contracts and resented Gabinius's conduct as governor of Syria.
A. Gabinius, returning from his governorship of Syria, faced furious hostility for having restored Ptolemy to Egypt without senatorial sanction; he was prosecuted on several counts in 54 BC.

notes.anchor: he is not barred by the lex Pupia
notes.note: The lex Pupia forbade Senate sittings on comitial days (assembly days), while the lex Gabinia required daily audience to be given to foreign embassies in February; Appius exploits the clash to keep the Senate busy and stall the elections.
Philistus of Syracuse, the Sicilian historian whom Quintus has been reading, modelled his dense, compressed style on Thucydides; Cicero rates him far above Callisthenes, the historian of Alexander.

notes.anchor: you shall have today's doings on the Lupercalia
notes.note: The Lupercalia, an ancient fertility festival held on 15 February, when half-naked runners (Luperci) struck bystanders with thongs; Cicero promises a news bulletin written that very day.
